9: The four lepers’ recognition of their obligation — yôm bᵉśôrāh hûʾ, ‘this day is a day of good news’ — is the OT’s clearest narrative application of the gospel-obligation. The lepers have news that will save the starving city; withholding it out of self-interest would be a punishable offense. The word bᵉśôrāh (good news, gospel) is the Hebrew root behind the LXX’s euangelion. Isaiah 52:7 (‘how beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news [mᵉḇaśśēr], who publishes peace, who brings good news of happiness’) applies this vocabulary to the herald of eschatological restoration; Paul in Romans 10:15 cites Isaiah 52:7 directly as the warrant for apostolic proclamation: pōs de kēryx sōsin ean mē apostalosōsin; kathōs gegraptai, hōs hōraioi hoi podes tōn euangelizomenōn agatha. The four lepers are the OT’s unexpected heralds — socially excluded, marginally positioned, but carriers of the word that saves. The pattern recurs in the resurrection: the first heralds of the empty tomb are women (Luke 24:9-10), socially undervalued as witnesses; the apostolic proclamation begins with the socially unlikely, as did the lepers’ announcement at the city gate. The lepers’ ‘this is a day of good news and we are keeping silent’ is the OT’s starkest statement of the obligation that attaches to received grace.
1: Elisha's word of impossible abundance: šimʿû dᵉḇar YHWH — hear the word of YHWH. kōh ʾāmar YHWH kāʿēt māḥār seʾāh sōlet bᵉšeqel ûsātayim śᵉʿōrîm bᵉšeqel bᵉšaʿar Šomrôn — tomorrow about this time a seah of fine flour for a shekel, and two seahs of barley for a shekel, at the gate of Samaria. Cheap food at the city gate within 24 hours — from the depths of siege-cannibalism famine (2 Kings 6:28-29) to market abundance. The specificity of the promise (next day, exact price, specific location) makes it prophetically falsifiable. Jesus's feeding of the five thousand (John 6) operates on similar prophetic-miraculous logic: impossible abundance given by the word of God's representative.
2: The king's officer (šālîš) on whose arm the king leaned responds with scorn: hinnēh YHWH ʿōśeh ʾărubbôt baššāmayim hăyihyeh haddāḇār hazzeh — “even if YHWH were to make windows in the heavens, could this thing happen?” The hypothetical of ʾărubbôt baššāmayim (floodgates/windows in the sky) echoes Genesis 7:11 (the opened windows of heaven at the Flood) as an impossibility benchmark. Elisha's response is judicial: hinnᵉḵā rōʾeh bᵉʿênêḵā wᵉmiššam lōʾ tōḵal — you will see it with your eyes but shall not eat of it. Unbelief is not disproven by seeing; it forecloses participation in the blessing. Thomas's unbelief (John 20:25) and the crowd's unbelief at the feeding (John 6:30) follow the same dynamic: they see but do not receive.
3: Four lepers sit at the entrance of the city gate: mᵉṣōrāʿîm hāyû pétaḥ-haššāʿar — they were excluded from the city by their leprosy (Leviticus 13:46). Their deliberation — lāmmāh ʾănaḥnû yōšᵉḇîm pōh ʿad mĕtnû — “Why are we sitting here until we die?” — is the pragmatic reasoning of the marginalized. The lepers are the last people anyone would expect to bring good news. They have no stake in either the city or the Aramean camp.
4: The lepers reason: if we go into the city, we die of famine; if we sit here, we die. wᵉʿattāh lᵉḵû wᵉnipᵉlāh ʾel maḥănēh ʾărām — so let us go over to the Aramean camp. If they let us live, we live; if they kill us, we die. The logic is pure desperation: death is certain either way, so try the option with the slim chance of survival. This pragmatic surrender to the enemy paradoxically becomes the mechanism of national deliverance. The lepers' willingness to go where no one else would go mirrors the pattern of unexpected instruments of salvation throughout Scripture.
5: The four lepers go to the edge of the Aramean camp at twilight (binesep hāʿareḇ): wayyāqûmû binesep hāʿareḇ lāḇôʾ ʾel maḥănēh ʾărām wayyāḇōʾû ʿad qᵉṣēh maḥănēh ʾărām wᵉhinnēh ʾên šām ʾîš — they came to the edge of the camp and behold, there was no one there. The discovery of the abandoned camp is a moment of pure wonder — the expected army is absent.
6: The theological explanation: wᵉʾădōnāy hišmîaʿ ʾet maḥănēh ʾărām qôl reḵeḵeḇ qôl sûs qôl ḥayil gādôl — the Lord had made the Aramean army hear a sound of chariots, horses, and a great army. They concluded (wayyōʾmᵉrû): Israel's king has hired the Hittites and Egyptians against us. The auditory miracle — YHWH creating the sound of a massive approaching army — panicked the Arameans into flight. The divine weapon is sound, not sword: YHWH fights the battle through confusion rather than force (cf. Judges 4:15; 2 Kings 7:22). The New Covenant equivalent is the sound of a mighty rushing wind at Pentecost (Acts 2:2) — YHWH's presence announced acoustically.
7: wayyāqûmû wayyānûsû bannesep wayyaʿazbû ʾet ʾōhᵉlêhem wᵉʾet sûsêhem wᵉʾet ḥămōrêhem hammahăneh kaʾăšer hîʾ wayyānūsû ʾel napšām — they rose and fled at twilight and abandoned their tents, horses, donkeys, and the camp as it was, and fled for their lives. The abandonment of everything — tents, horses, donkeys — in haste to flee recalls the Exodus plunder (Exodus 12:36): Israel went out with Egyptian wealth that the Egyptians left behind in their urgency. The Samaritan lepers now find what Israel found at the Exodus — abundance left by the departing enemy.
8: The lepers enter the camp: wayyāḇōʾû hamᵉṣōrāʿîm hāʾēlleh ʾad qᵉṣēh hammahăneh wayyāḇōʾû ʾel-ʾōhel ʾeḥāt wayyōḵlû wayyišᵉtû wayyiśʾû miššam kesef wᵉzāhāḇ ûḇᵉgādîm wayyēlᵉḵû wayyaṭmᵉnû — they eat, drink, carry off silver, gold, and garments, and hide them. The lepers' first response to found treasure is personal consumption and hoarding. This is the natural human response to unexpected abundance. But their conscience brings them back to the need to share the news.
10: The lepers go to the city gatekeepers and report: bāʾnû ʾel maḥănēh ʾărām wᵉhinnēh ʾên šām ʾîš wᵉqôl ʾādām kî ʾim sûs ʾāsûr wāḥămôr ʾāsûr wᵉʾōhālîm kᵉʾăšer hēm — we came to the Aramean camp and there was no one there, no human voice — only horses and donkeys tied, and tents as they were. The announcement from the city gate echoes Isaiah 52:7: mah-nāwû ʿal hehārîm raglê mᵉḇaśśēr — “how beautiful on the mountains are the feet of the one who brings good news.” The lepers become evangelists — their feet carry the good news first to the city gate, then through the gatekeepers to the king.
11: The gatekeepers call out (wayyiqrᵉʾû haššōʿᵉrîm wayyaggîdû bêt hammélek pᵉnîmāh) and report to the royal palace. The chain of communication — lepers to gatekeepers to king — mirrors the pattern of gospel spread: the message originates with unlikely witnesses and propagates through institutional channels. The Samaritan woman (John 4) follows the same pattern: she who was least expected brings the news of the Messiah to her whole city.
12: The king rises at night and suspects a trap: ʾaggîdāh nāʾ lāḵem ʾēt ʾăšer ʿāśû lānû ʾărām — “I will tell you what the Arameans have done to us.” He knows we are hungry; they have abandoned the camp to draw us out; when we go, they will ambush us. The king's paranoia — interpreting miraculous provision as a military ruse — is theologically significant: he cannot receive grace without suspecting manipulation. The instinct to interpret divine gift as trap is a form of theological paralysis.
13: A servant suggests sending five horses to check: even if they die, it is no worse than the people dying in the city. The pragmatic argument — the risk of verification is no worse than the cost of inaction — persuades the king. Two chariot teams are sent (wayyiqḥû šᵉnê reḵeḇ sûsîm). The willingness to risk a small amount to verify the promised salvation parallels the demand for evidence before belief that recurs in the NT (John 20:25; Matthew 27:40-42).
14: The two chariot teams go to the Jordan following the trail of discarded garments and equipment (bᵉgādîm ûḵēlîm ʾăšer hišlîḵû ʾărām bᵉḥipzām) left by the fleeing Arameans. The trail of abandoned equipment confirms the lepers' report. The physical evidence — clothes and gear strewn on the road — makes the miraculous flight visible and traceable. The abandoned equipment is the material residue of divine intervention.
15: The messengers return and report to the king. The people go out and plunder the Aramean camp: wayyiśʾû hāʿām wayyābōzzû ʾet maḥănēh ʾărām — and the people went out and plundered the Aramean camp. wayhî seʾāh sōlet bᵉšeqel ûsātayim śᵉʿōrîm bᵉšeqel kidbar YHWH — a seah of fine flour for a shekel and two seahs of barley for a shekel, according to the word of YHWH. The formula of prophetic fulfillment (kidbar YHWH) is the narrator's emphatic claim: the specific price prophecy of v1 has been fulfilled exactly. No word of YHWH fails.
16: The people go out and plunder: wayyābōzzû ʾet maḥănēh ʾărām — they plundered the Aramean camp. The goods of the defeated enemy flow to the besieged city — a complete reversal of the siege economy. Famine to abundance within one day fulfills Elisha's prophecy (v1). The reversal is so rapid and so complete that it can only be attributed to YHWH's direct action. The same sudden reversal of fortunes — from desperate hunger to miraculous provision — characterizes the feeding of the five thousand (John 6:11) and the eschatological banquet (Isaiah 25:6-8).
17: The king appoints the unbelieving officer (from v2) to manage the city gate — and the people trampled him in the gate and he died (wayyirmeśû-ʾōtô hāʿām baššaʿar wayyāmōt). This fulfills Elisha's prophetic word in v2: hinnᵉḵā rōʾeh bᵉʿênêḵā wᵉmiššam lōʾ tōḵal — you will see it with your eyes but not eat of it. The officer sees the abundance but does not participate in it — trampled at the very gate where good news arrives. Unbelief in the face of prophetic promise does not prevent the fulfillment; it only excludes the unbeliever from participating in the blessing.
18: The narrator restates Elisha's prophecy from v1 verbatim for the fulfillment-confirmation formula: wayhî kidbar ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm ʾăšer dibber ʾel hammélek — it came to pass according to the word of the man of God that he spoke to the king. The double narration (prophecy + fulfillment notation) is the DH's standard technique for prophetic fulfillment certification. Every major prophecy in Kings receives this confirmation stamp.
19: The officer's doubting speech is restated: wᵉhinnēh YHWH ʿōśeh ʾărubbôt baššāmayim hăyihyeh kaddāḇār hazzeh — even if YHWH made windows in the heaven, could this be? This repetition creates the fulfillment-confirmation structure: the exact words of unbelief are quoted again to show they have been answered. The narrative technique highlights that prophetic words — and doubting responses — are remembered and evaluated against what actually happened.
20: wayyehî-lô ḵēn wayyirmeśû-ʾōtô hāʿām baššaʿar wayyāmōt — and so it happened to him; the people trampled him in the gate and he died. The double fulfillment — abundance arrived (positive), unbeliever trampled (negative) — forms a bipartite prophetic fulfillment: the word of YHWH produces both salvation and judgment simultaneously. The same structure operates at Jesus's first coming: his words produce both resurrection and condemnation (John 5:24-29), both the opened eyes of the blind and the confirmed blindness of those who claim to see (John 9:39).
